text: Over the last 8 years of trading, I've
tested just about everything.
Indicators, patterns, scalping systems,
supply and demand, order flow,
breakouts. And while a lot of those
things work, there is one setup that
completely changed the way that I trade.
In fact, if you took away everything
from me today, and told me I could only
trade one setup for the rest of my life,
this would probably be it. It's called
the 30-minute opening range breakout.
And the reason I love it is because it's
very simple. There's no guessing, no
overtrading, no staring at charts all
day. I trade for just about like 90
minutes every single morning, and then I
call it a day. So, today I'm going to
show you exactly how I trade the
30-minute orb, why it works, where most
traders mess it up, and how you can
start using it yourself. As always,
before we hop into the video, if you do

let's go ahead and let's hop into the
video. All right, so let's start with
why this setup works. Every single
morning at 9:30 a.m. EST, the market
opens. This is where the majority of
volume is going to start entering the
market. Institutions are active, funds
are active, algorithms are active.
Everybody is reacting to overnight news,
overnight earnings, economic data in the
pre-market, and positioning. So, because
of that, the first 30 minutes often
creates a range that becomes extremely
important for the rest of the day. Think
of it like a battlefield. The first 30
minutes shows us where these buyers and
sellers are fighting. Once one side
wins, we often get a strong directional
move, whether it be to the upside or to
the downside. Now, our job isn't to
predict who's going to win this battle,
but our job is to react when we actually
break the range. So, here is step number
one. We want to mark the range. So, at
10:00 a.m. EST, the first thing I do is
mark the high and the low of the first
30 minutes. I'll show you guys exactly
how to do that here in a little bit, but
that's it. Nothing complicated. I draw
one line at the high, one line at the
low. Now I have my opening range. The
market has essentially told me these are
the key levels for today. Now from
there, all I do is wait. So now if we
were actually to plot this on the chart.
So we are going to find our 9:30 a.m.
candle and then our 9:55 a.m. candle
because 9:55 ends at 10:00 a.m. So the
next candle opens at 10:00 a.m. So
again, we want to find the highest point
of that 9:30 candle and then the lowest
point of that 9:55 candle or, you know,
vice versa. Either way, the highest and
lowest point of those two levels. And
all we are simply going to do is we are
going to take a line and we are going to
draw it against the lows and we are
going to draw it against the highs. Now
this is going to be our battlefield and
we want to wait for price to break and
close outside of this range on a
5-minute candle. So as we see, yes, we
poked below it right here, but did we
close below it on the 5-minute? No. Did
we close below it here on the 5-minute?
Yes, indeed we did. So therefore, this
is a bearish break and we should be
looking for shorts, which actually leads
us into step number two, which is not
chasing the breakout. All right? This is
where most traders lose money. Price
breaks the range, they get excited, they
jump in immediately, then the market
reverses and stops them out. I never
chase the initial breakout. Instead, I
always wait for confirmation. I want to
see a candle close outside of the range.
I don't want a wick, I don't want a
touch. We need to have a 5-minute candle
close. So remember, wicks do the damage,
but bodies tell the story. Once I get
the close outside of the range, now I'm
interested. Now step number three is to
wait for the retest. Now this is where
the magic happens. Once price breaks
outside of the range, closes outside of
it on the 5-minute time frame, I want to
see it come back and retest the breakout
level. So, if we broke above the
30-minute range, I want to see price
come back down and retest that 30-minute
high as support. If we break below the
30-minute range, I want to see price
come back up and retest it as
resistance. So, we're going long, price
breaks above the opening range high,
price pulls back, test that level, and
it holds, and we start to see buyers
coming in, that's our confirmation to go
long. Same thing for shorts. And that is
what I call the break and retest of the
30-minute opening range. And honestly,
it's one of the easiest setups that you
will ever find. Because now, we're using
a level that the entire market can see.
